elements.sent-2411-001: Rydberg atom arrays with Van der Waals interactions provide a controllable path to simulate the locally connected transverse-field Ising model (TFIM), a prototypical model in statistical mechanics.
elements.sent-2411-002: Remotely operating the publicly accessible Aquila Rydberg atom array, we experimentally investigate the physics of TFIM far from equilibrium and uncover significant deviations from the theoretical predictions.
elements.sent-2411-003: Rather than the expected ballistic spread of correlations, the Rydberg simulator exhibits a sub-ballistic spread, along with a logarithmic scaling of entanglement entropy in time — all while the system mostly retains its initial magnetization.
elements.sent-2411-004: By modeling the atom motion in tweezer traps, we trace these effects to an emergent natural disorder in Rydberg atom arrays, which we characterize with a minimal random spin model.
elements.sent-2411-005: We further experimentally explore the different dynamical regimes hosted in the system by varying the lattice spacing and the Rabi frequency.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our findings highlight the crucial role of atom motion in the many-body dynamics of Rydberg atom arrays at the TFIM limit, and propose simple benchmark measurements to test for its presence in future experiments.
elements.sent-2411-007: A particularly intriguing aspect of Rydberg atom arrays interacting with Van der Waals (vdW) interactions is the possibility of realizing the prototypical model of quantum statistical mechanics: the transverse field Ising model (TFIM).
elements.sent-2411-008: Besides exhibiting a quantum phase transition \cite{sachdev2001quantum , TFIM also has rich behavior when driven out of equilibrium \cite{PhysRevA.69.053616,PhysRevLett.106.227203,Calabrese_2012,PhysRevLett.121.016801,PhysRevLett.123.115701,2020arXiv200412287D,PhysRevB.107.L121113 .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: One nonequilibrium behavior of TFIM is the ballistic spread of correlations when it is quenched, which originates from the constant propagation speed of the excitations generated due to the quench \cite{Calabrese_2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: This also leads to a volume-law entangled state with a linearly increasing entanglement entropy in time \cite{Calabrese_Entropy .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Here we report on the experimental observation of instead a sub-ballistic propagation in a one-dimensional Rydberg atom array under a quench [Figure \ref{Fig1 (a)], which subsequently generates sub-volume law entangled states with logarithmic increase of entanglement entropy in time against the theoretical prediction.}

elements.sent-2411-014: Our tensor network simulations instead, trace this unexpected phenomenon to the motion of atoms that are trapped in tweezers, Fig.~\ref{Fig0 .}

elements.sent-2411-016: We then detect localized and thermal regimes in which the initial magnetization is either retained or lost, accompanied by a logarithmic or fast increase in quantum Fisher information (QFI), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-017: Inspired by the experimental data, we introduce a minimal random spin model to describe the physics of the Rydberg atom array when it is mapped to TFIM, which can be utilized in future theoretical studies.

elements.sent-2411-061: Minimal random spin model.~Inspired by these experimental observations and to gain more insight about the role of atom motion in many-body dynamics, we write a minimal yet realistic many-body model for the TFIM limit of the Rydberg atom arrays.
elements.sent-2411-062: This time-independent model incorporates only the effect of atom motion into the Hamiltonian with the uncertainty in the atom positions $\delta r$, \begin{eqnarray}H^{\rm eff} = \sum_r J_r \sigma^z_r \sigma^z_{r+1} + \frac{\Omega}{2} \sum_r\sigma^x_r - \sum_r h_r \sigma^z_r, \label{eq:emergentSpin} J_r = \mathcal{N}\left( \frac{1}{4}\frac{C_6}{a^6},\frac{3}{2}\frac{C_6 \delta r}{a^7} \right), h_r = \mathcal{N}\left(0, 3\frac{C_6 \delta r}{a^7}\right),\end{eqnarray} where $\mathcal{N (\mu_N,\sigma_N)$ stand for normal distribution with mean $\mu_N$ and standard deviation $\sigma_N$.}

elements.sent-2411-096: These results show that it is possible to take on a trace of $Z_2$ SSB transition in an experimental data of a disordered many-body system pushed out of equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-097: Conclusions and Outlook.~Our work experimentally demonstrates the remarkable effect of atom motion in quantum many-body dynamics on a Rydberg simulator, and provides a minimal model that captures these observations.
elements.sent-2411-098: This model can be used to predict the many-body dynamics of Rydberg atom arrays under a protocol that quenches the system to its TFIM limit.
elements.sent-2411-099: While different disordered Ising models are present in the literature \cite{PhysRevB.88.014206,PhysRevLett.113.107204,PhysRevB.104.115159 , Hamiltonian Eq.~\eqref{eq:emergentSpin is yet to be fully explored in theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Importantly, can introducing decoherence to the minimal model modify the predicted random disorder profile with respect to atom distance? If decoherence is responsible for the mismatch between full simulation and experiment around $a=7.5\mu$m, why chains with different lattice spacings decohere differently, and whether the effect of decoherence is maximized due to a nonequilibrium criticality \cite{fang2024probingcriticalphenomenaopen are interesting questions to answer in the future.}
elements.sent-2411-101: TFIM limit is rather a fine-tuned point on a Rydberg atom array, which explains the importance of atom motion in many-body dynamics at this limit.
elements.sent-2411-102: Our results imply more generally the fine-tuned points in other platforms \cite{RevModPhys.93.025001,richerme2014non,guo2024site , can be susceptible to particle motion.}
elements.sent-2411-103: While we exclusively discussed ferromagnetic initial states due to simplicity, the atom motion also dominates for quenches from Neel states, and possibly for slow ramps, to the low energy sector of the Ising Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-104: This suggests that more advanced cooling techniques are needed to faithfully realize Ising models with only transverse field on Rydberg simulators.
elements.sent-2411-105: Alternatively, it is an interesting question to ask whether one can synchronize the atoms in tweezers \cite{samoylova2015synchronization to combat the emergent disorder.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Our work sets simple benchmarking experiments far from equilibrium with spatio-temporal spread of correlations, quantum Fisher information, magnetization and domain-wall density to test whether the atom motion is sufficiently suppressed in quantum many-body dynamics.. elements.sent-2411-115: We calibrate our measurements before any many-body measurement by doing a two-photon resonance experiment on a collection of independent atoms on 2D geometry.
elements.sent-2411-116: In this experiment, we suddenly quench the Rabi frequency from 0 to the desired Rabi frequency, $\Omega$, keep the detuning constant at a value $\Delta$ and make a measurement at time $t_{\pi =\pi/\Omega$.}
elements.sent-2411-117: We repeat the same measurement for different detuning values, and obtain the experimental excitation probability with respect to detuning.
elements.sent-2411-118: The excitation probability can be analytically derived via quantum optics techniques \cite{meystre2007elements , \begin{eqnarray}P_e(\Delta) = \frac{\Omega^2}{\Omega^2+\Delta^2} \sin^2\left(\sqrt{\Omega^2+\Delta^2} \frac{t_{\pi}}{2}\right). \notag\end{eqnarray} We fit our experimental data to $P_e(\Delta)$ to find the systematic global detuning shift and the calibrated Rabi frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-119: All reported values in the Letter are calibrated values.
elements.sent-2411-120: We then correct for the systematic detuning shift in all many-body measurements done in the same day.
elements.sent-2411-121: Let us emphasize the importance of the calibration experiment before many-body measurements at the TFIM limit.
elements.sent-2411-122: Since TFIM limit is a fine-tuned point in detuning, it is crucial to correct for this systematic uncertainty rather than modeling it in numerics.
elements.sent-2411-123: Calibration experiment also provides us 1-$\sigma$ deviation from the measured values.
elements.sent-2411-124: We apply bootstrap technique \cite{bootstrap_ana to first observe the emergence of normal distribution for calibrated Rabi frequency and detuning, and then to extract 1-$\sigma$ deviation.}
elements.sent-2411-125: The latter is used in numerical modeling of Rabi frequency and detuning fluctuations from shot-to-shot.
elements.sent-2411-126: We also locally apply bootstrap technique to each atom to determine the spatial detuning profile across the many-body chain, and find that this is roughly sinusoidal across the array \cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Based on this method, we find negligible differences in the Rabi frequency of the atoms, and hence we do not include spatial variations in Rabi frequency in our numerical modeling.
elements.sent-2411-128: We apply error mitigation to the many-body data with confusion matrices derived for one- and two-body observables incorporating the readout errors \cite{PhysRevA.103.042605,supp .}
elements.sent-2411-129: In addition to that, we postselect the final bit strings based on the correct initial state, which is the state where all atoms are in their ground state.

elements.sent-2411-239: Conclusions and Outlook.~This work experimentally demonstrates the remarkable effect of atom motion in quantum many-body dynamics on a Rydberg atom array at its blockade radius and provides a minimal model that captures the experimental observations.
elements.sent-2411-240: The minimal model encodes the atom motion as disorder in Ising interactions and longitudinal field, which differs from the random Ising models in the literature \cite{PhysRevB.88.014206,PhysRevLett.113.107204,PhysRevB.104.115159 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: Hence we show that Rydberg atom arrays could be used to study the physics of disordered many-body systems without the need for local detuning control.
elements.sent-2411-242: This work also demonstrates the subtle interplay between atom motion and decoherence processes by clearly differentiating their contribution to the many-body dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-243: We further find that the decoherence effects are enhanced when the Hamiltonian is close to a quantum critical point.
elements.sent-2411-244: TFIM limit is a fine-tuned point on a Rydberg atom array, which explains the importance of atom motion at this limit.
elements.sent-2411-245: Our results imply, more generally, that the fine-tuned points in other platforms \cite{RevModPhys.93.025001,richerme2014non,guo2024site , can be susceptible to particle motion.}
elements.sent-2411-246: While we exclusively discussed ferromagnetic initial states, the atom motion also dominates for quenches from Neel states, and possibly for slow ramps, to the low-energy sector of the Ising Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-247: This suggests that more advanced cooling techniques are needed to realize clean Ising models with only transverse field on Rydberg systems.
elements.sent-2411-248: It may be interesting to consider whether the atoms can be synchronized \cite{samoylova2015synchronization to combat the disorder.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Quantizing the atom motion and considering the full atom-phonon Hamiltonian would take into account the backaction of the Rydberg interactions on the atomic motion, and hence can be an interesting future direction for determining the role of the atom-phonon entanglement in the many-body dynamics of Rydberg atom arrays.
elements.sent-2411-250: Our work characterizes a naturally disordered limit of Rydberg atom arrays with simple benchmarking experiments far from equilibrium, and demonstrates how to harness atom motion as a resource for exploring the physics of disordered many-body systems.. elements.sent-2411-296: Hence, the sub-ballistic lightcone behavior observed in Ref.~\cite{PhysRevLett.132.133401 when the phonon state was prepared in $\ket{1}$, must originate from a physics different from the disorder.}
elements.sent-2411-297: Therefore, the possible localization phenomena that can be observed in Rydberg atom arrays \cite{bernien2017probing,PhysRevLett.132.133401 seem to originate from different sources, demonstrating the richness of the physics of this platform while differentiating the emergence of disorder we found in this work from the literature.}

elements.sent-2411-301: We also observe in Fig.~\ref{SMFig8 that adding the fitted decoherence level to the MM does not have an effect on the QFI density.}
elements.sent-2411-302: However, decoherence slightly changes the magnetization, domain-wall density, and entanglement entropy.
elements.sent-2411-303: In fact, the behaviors of atom motion and decoherence on the many-body dynamics are distinct in these observables: atom motion stabilizes magnetization and domain-wall density, whereas decoherence causes a tendency in them to decay.
elements.sent-2411-304: For entanglement entropy, atom motion leads to logarithmic increase in time, whereas adding decoherence leads to linear increase in time.
elements.sent-2411-305: It is not unusual to see the effect of the same decoherence in different observables differently, as some observables are more susceptible to the decoherence effects.
elements.sent-2411-306: All in all, we conclude that the experimental data in Fig.~(2) in the main text are not significantly affected by the decoherence processes in the experimental setup.
